# Literature

Research shows that gender and institutional structures shape key aspects of academic work, from team formation to curriculum design. Four strands of this literature are particularly relevant to our study: (i) research on how gender influences collaboration patterns, (ii) the representation of women in course materials, (iii) the interdisciplinarity of mixed-gender teams, and (iv) the propensity of such teams to innovate. These insights provide the conceptual foundation for our study, which we review in detail below.

## Gender and team formation in academia

Teams play an increasingly important role in knowledge production [@wuchty2007increasing; @milojevic2014principles] and a large literature documents strong *gender homophily* in scientific collaboration and professional networks [@mcpherson2001birds]. Scholars in economics, the life sciences, and many other fields tend to co-author disproportionately with same-gender colleagues [@mcdowell1992effect; @holman2019researchers; @boschini2007team; @kwiek2021gender]. Notably, this form of gender-based sorting is not merely driven by institutional constraints: it appears even in large and diverse research environments [e.g., @kwiek2021gender] and across career stages [e.g., @nguyen2025mind]. Such gender homophily is also considered a contributing factor to gender gaps in outputs [e.g., @ductor2023gender] and to broader gender inequalities in academia [e.g., @mcdowell1992effect]. 

While research collaborations have been widely investigated, collaboration patterns in teaching remain less well understood. On one hand, homophily may shape teaching teams through the same individual preferences observed in research collaborations. On the other, teaching has distinctive features: its predominantly local and in-person nature could either constrain or amplify gender-based homophily. These considerations motivate our effort to explore the prevalence and gender composition of teaching teams at scale.

## Representation of Women in Citations and Course materials

A substantial body of research examines gender differences in citation patterns [@dion2018gendered; @ductor2023gender; @mishra2018self; @card2022gender], driving factor of the persistent gender bias in academia [@boivin2024sexism]. Citation practices often reflect a form of gender homophily [e.g., @card2022gender] and male-dominated fields may confer a “first-mover advantage” to men [e.g., @newman2009first]. Other contributing factors include different self-citation patterns [@mishra2018self] and overall gaps in publication output [@ductor2023gender].  

Evidence that these disparities extend into teaching is more limited, but studies indicate that female scholars are underrepresented in course syllabi [@harris2020diversify; @colgan2017gender; @skitka2021gender]. This underrepresentation appears linked to the instructor's gender. For example, in Political Science, instructors' gender predicts the assignment of female-authored readings, suggesting a form of gender homophily in course content [@smith2020gender]. Similarly, in research on political science, @dion2018gendered finds that female-led teams cite more women, whereas mixed-gender teams do not cite more women than all-male teams. These findings suggest that team composition and dynamics strongly influence decisions about what to cite or include in course readings. A key goal of our study is to investigate these patterns on a large scale across multiple domains and institutions.

## Mixed-Gender Teams and Interdisciplinarity

Another relevant strand of literature concerns the drivers of interdisciplinary work in research. Recent evidence suggests that women, on average, undertake more interdisciplinary research than men [@pinheiro2022women], and that interdisciplinary collaborations can produce broader scientific outcomes [@specht2022interdisciplinary]. Taken together, this work suggests that the presence of women in a team is associated with more interdisciplinary intellectual contributions. 

In a teaching context, however, other factors may influence this relationship. For example, being interdisciplinary in resarch does not necessiarly translte into interdisciplinary teaching styles. Furtheormore, students may not demand for interdisciplinary courses. Or there might be institutional constraints where programs are not designed to support interdisciplinarity. Finally, also team dynamics or matching patterns, may prevent instructors to find good partners for interdisciplinary courses. To shed lights on these issues, we explored at a large scale the relationships between course interdisciplinarity, team size, and gender composition in teaching.

## Gender Diversity and Innovation

Extensive research has shown that gender-diverse teams tend to generate more novel and higher-impact ideas than single-gender teams in scientific collaboration [e.g., @yang2022gender]. Related work on research and R&D teams links gender diversity to more radical or innovative outputs, as well as to improved decision-making and performance [@apesteguia2012impact; @opstrup2015right; @bear2011role; @campbell2013gender; @love2022impact]. 

However, in the context of teaching, the relationship between gender diversity and "novelty" is less well understood. A key challenge lies in defining measures of novelty in a pedagogical setting. First, syllabi often rely heavily on textbooks rather than journal articles, and pedagogical innovation may diverge from scientific frontier. Moreover, innovation in teaching can take different forms: instructors can adopt new methods that are pedagogically novel but not at the scientific frontier (“teaching novelty”); they can assign more recent materials (“recency”); or they can include papers at the forefront of research (“scientific novelty”). In this study, we propose simple measures to begin exploring these three dimensions empirically and at scale.

## Covid-19's impact on academic work 

Our work is also related to research on the impact of COVID-19 on academic work. Research documents that during COVID there was a decrease in research time, project initiation, and scientific productivity, with particularly strong effects for women and researchers with caregiving responsibilities [@myers2020unequal; @viglione2020women; @gao2021potentially;@cui2022gender].^[
    Although the effect of lockdowns and restricted mobility on productivity is found to be country- and field-dependent [@garcia2024silver].
]
At the same time, bibliometric studies show that the crisis reshaped collaboration networks: for instance, a decrease in new co-authorships [@gao2021potentially] and changes in the geographical distribution of collaboration, including an increase in cross-border teaming [@venturini2025effectsremoteworkingscientific]. Commentaries on scientific meetings emphasise that the pandemic-driven shift to virtual conferences reshaped scientific networking: while online formats broadened participation — including a modest increase in women’s representation [@olechnicka2025impact] — they also reduced opportunities for informal, in-person interactions that are seen as vital for collaboration and idea generation [@bray2022together].

In parallel, universities were forced into a rapid transition to online and hybrid teaching, potentially altering how teaching teams are formed and how often instructors collaborate across gender and disciplinary boundaries. We therefore exploit the onset of COVID-19 as a quasi-exogenous shock and estimate an event study around 2020 to examine whether the prevalence and composition of mixed-gender teams changed after the pandemic, conditional on institution and field.
